entry.title: SAP Sapphire 2026 - SAP Autonomous Suite (What It Means for Consultants, Clients, and Executives)
entry.content:
Why SAP Autonomous Suite is different from consumer-grade generative AI

Most business users now understand generative AI through consumer-grade AI assistants. A user asks a question, gets a response, refines the prompt, and uses the output to write an email, summarize a document, build code, or analyze an idea. That experience is powerful, but it is mainly individual productivity AI.

SAP Autonomous Suite is different because it is designed for enterprise process execution. A person using a general-purpose AI tool may ask, “Can you summarize why this invoice is blocked?” The response may be helpful, but an enterprise finance team needs much more than a good explanation. It needs the system to know the vendor, purchase order, goods receipt, tax rule, tolerance limit, approval workflow, segregation-of-duties policy, and posting controls. It also needs traceability, authorization, auditability, and controlled execution. [3], [4]

That is where SAP’s business context becomes meaningful. Joule Assistants and Joule Agents are designed to operate with process context, not just language context. The difference is not simply a better answer. The difference is whether the system can help move a business process forward safely. [3], [7]

What SAP Autonomous Suite means for consultants

For consultants, SAP Autonomous Suite changes the nature of advisory work. The consultant’s role will not be limited to configuring transactions, preparing functional specifications, or building integrations. Consultants will increasingly need to design autonomous process patterns.

A traditional consultant may ask, “What transaction does the user execute, and what fields are required?” An autonomous-suite consultant must ask, “What business outcome is the user trying to achieve, what decisions are repetitive, what context is required, what can be automated, what must be approved, and what should remain inside the clean core?”

This creates new consulting responsibilities. Consultants must distinguish automation from autonomy, decompose end-to-end processes into decision points, and design trust controls such as role-based access, confidence thresholds, audit logs, exception routing, human-in-the-loop approvals, and clean-core write-back. [7]

What SAP Autonomous Suite means for clients

For clients, SAP Autonomous Suite should not be viewed as another feature upgrade. It should be viewed as a new operating model for business processes. Many enterprises still run processes that depend heavily on manual coordination across procurement, supply chain, finance, warehouse operations, and customer service. Each team may use SAP, but people still connect the dots manually.

The best starting points are business processes with high volume, repeatable decisions, measurable delays, and clear governance rules. Strong candidates include invoice processing, financial close, reconciliation, purchase requisitions, supplier selection, fulfillment exceptions, inventory imbalance, employee service requests, and customer service case handling.

The success factor for clients is readiness. Autonomous processes require clean data, stable master data, harmonized rules, well-defined APIs, cloud readiness, process ownership, and change management. AI will not fix a broken process; it will expose it faster.

What SAP Autonomous Suite means for executives

For executives, SAP Autonomous Suite should be understood as a business transformation lever, not a technical add-on. The executive question is not “How many AI agents can we deploy?” The better question is “Which business outcomes can we improve by redesigning work around intelligent, governed execution?” [1], [7]

For a CFO, this may mean reducing the financial close cycle, improving working capital, reducing invoice exceptions, and increasing audit confidence. For a COO or Chief Supply Chain Officer, it may mean reducing fulfillment delays, improving service levels, predicting supply disruptions earlier, and improving logistics productivity. For a CIO, it means building the platform foundation: clean core, BTP, Integration Suite, data governance, security, identity, observability, and responsible AI controls. [1], [7]

Real-world example 1: JK Cement and AI-driven purchase requisitions

A useful real-world example is JK Cement Ltd., one of India’s major cement producers. The company had a high-volume procurement environment with thousands of daily transactions, including more than a thousand purchase requisitions per day and thousands of invoices. Purchase requisition creation involved manual effort, complex screens, and multiple fields. [5]

Using SAP BTP and SAP Business AI, JK Cement created a conversational experience where employees could type purchase requests in plain English. The solution interprets the request, applies business logic, and creates the purchase requisition in SAP S/4HANA through governed integration. SAP reported 73% efficiency achieved in creating purchase requisitions and 50% greater time savings in purchase-related processes. [5]

This example shows the direction of SAP Autonomous Suite clearly. The user is not navigating a transaction manually. The user is expressing intent. The AI-enabled system interprets that intent, connects with enterprise data, applies business rules, and completes a structured business process. [5]

Consultant lesson: The work is not only chatbot design. It is process redesign, authorization design, integration design, validation logic, fallback handling, and change management.

Client lesson: Start with a high-volume process where users spend too much time entering structured information.

Executive lesson: The value is not only time savings. It is a scalable model for simplifying work across departments.

Real-world example 2: Western Sugar and autonomous invoice processing

Another strong example is Western Sugar Cooperative, which used SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to improve supplier invoice processing. Western Sugar handles approximately 40,000 supplier invoices annually. Many invoices arrived in paper or PDF format, creating manual processing effort, delays, and payment risk for growers and suppliers. [6]

SAP reported that Western Sugar processes 40,000 supplier invoices without human intervention and reduced invoice processing time by 25%. The AI capability embedded in SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management helps process invoices faster, enabling the accounts payable team to focus more time on higher-value finance work. [6]

This is a practical illustration of autonomy in finance. The process is not simply digitizing an invoice. The system captures invoice data, validates it, routes it, supports approval, and posts it after approval. Human involvement is preserved where it matters, especially for approval and exception handling, but routine effort is reduced. [6]

Consultant lesson: Finance autonomy depends on process controls, tolerance rules, exception logic, and integration with the general ledger.

Client lesson: Invoice processing is a strong starting point because it has volume, measurable cycle time, and clear business impact.

Executive lesson: Autonomous finance is not just about reducing manual work. It improves control, visibility, supplier trust, and working capital discipline.

A practical architecture view

A practical SAP Autonomous Suite architecture can be understood through five layers: business execution, process and integration, data and context, intelligence, and governance. The clean-core principle remains essential. The ERP core should continue to handle transactional integrity: postings, ATP, pricing, delivery, invoicing, approvals, and financial records. The intelligence layer should reason, recommend, classify, summarize, and orchestrate. Write-back should happen only through approved APIs, workflows, and governed extensions. [1], [3], [7]

 

Final thoughts

SAP Autonomous Suite is not just another AI announcement. It is a signal that enterprise software is entering a new phase.

For consultants, this means learning how to design AI-enabled process patterns, not just configure applications. For clients, it means selecting business processes where autonomy can deliver measurable value without compromising control. For executives, it means treating AI as an operating model transformation, not a productivity experiment.

The most successful enterprises will not be the ones that deploy the most agents. They will be the ones that know where autonomy belongs, where human judgment must remain, and how to govern both within a clean, secure, and scalable SAP architecture.

SAP Autonomous Suite therefore represents a practical evolution of enterprise transformation: from transactions to outcomes, from automation to autonomy, and from isolated AI experiments to governed enterprise intelligence. [1], [7]

References

[1] SAP News Center, “SAP Unveils the Autonomous Enterprise,” 2026. URL: https://news.sap.com/2026/05/sap-sapphire-sap-unveils-autonomous-enterprise/

[2] SAP, “SAP Sapphire 2026 Innovation News Guide,” 2026. URL: https://www.sap.com/topics/events/sapphire/innovation-news-guide-2026

[3] SAP, “Joule Agents and Joule Assistants,” 2026. URL: https://www.sap.com/products/artificial-intelligence/ai-agents.html

[4] SAP, “Joule Business AI Solutions,” 2026. URL: https://www.sap.com/products/artificial-intelligence.html

[5] SAP, “JK Cement: Laying a scalable foundation for fast, intuitive processes driven by intelligent automation with SAP Business AI,” 2025. URL: https://www.sap.com/asset/dynamic/2025/09/72b8b6e8-1f7f-0010-bca6-c68f7e60039b.html

[6] SAP, “Western Sugar: Automating invoice processing with AI to keep growers and suppliers super sweet,” 2025. URL: https://www.sap.com/asset/dynamic/2025/02/4282b6dc-f67e-0010-bca6-c68f7e60039b.html

[7] SAP News Center, “SAP Unveils Business AI Platform to Power the Autonomous Enterprise,” 2026. URL: https://news.sap.com/2026/05/sap-sapphire-keynote-business-ai-platform-power-autonomous-enterprise/

entry.title: Factoring AI into Your Procurement Strategy
entry.content:
Driving Real Procurement Transformation: Beyond Software and AI 🌐

In a recent episode of SAP’s Future of Procurement podcast, Jay Brand, Director of Solution Advisory for Spend and Finance, shared candid insights into what truly drives successful procurement transformations. While technology—especially AI—dominates today’s headlines, the conversation underscored a critical truth: transformation is not delivered by software alone. It is achieved through clarity of purpose, strong leadership, disciplined execution, and ongoing commitment to improvement. 🚀

Software Isn’t Magic—Strategy Comes First 🧭

A central theme in the discussion was the misconception that software alone can deliver transformation. Organizations often invest heavily in technology expecting immediate results, only to be disappointed when outcomes fall short. As Brand highlighted, tools are only as effective as the strategy behind them.

True transformation starts with a clear understanding of business objectives, measurable KPIs, and the organizational will to change. Without this foundation, even the most advanced solutions become underutilized. Leaders must define the “why” before investing in the “how,” ensuring alignment across stakeholders and a shared vision for success. 📊

Avoiding the Feature Trap ⚙️

Another common pitfall is the tendency to get lost in feature-level comparisons during solution evaluation. Teams often focus on minor system differences—interface preferences or configuration details—rather than assessing how a platform supports broader business goals.

Brand emphasized that this “feature-first” mindset can derail transformation efforts. Instead, organizations should prioritize outcomes over perfection, avoiding analysis paralysis. Decision-making should be streamlined, guided by a small group of empowered stakeholders, and focused on achieving measurable value rather than replicating legacy processes. 🔍

Transformation Is a Continuous Journey 🌱

One of the most compelling analogies shared during the podcast likened transformation to caring for a plant. Organizations invest heavily during implementation—watering, nurturing, and monitoring progress. However, once systems go live, attention often fades, leading to stagnation or failure.

Successful companies recognize that transformation doesn’t end at go-live. Continuous improvement, regular optimization, and ongoing user engagement are critical. Cloud solutions, in particular, require iterative enhancements, frequent reviews, and adaptation to evolving business needs. Maintaining this momentum ensures long-term value realization. 🔄

AI: Powerful, but Not a Shortcut 🤖

The rise of artificial intelligence has introduced both opportunity and unrealistic expectations. While AI holds significant promise for procurement, Brand stressed that it is not a “magic bullet.” Organizations expecting AI to instantly solve complex challenges often overlook the foundational work required.

Effective AI adoption begins with clearly defined problems. Companies must articulate specific use cases rather than asking broad questions like “What can AI do for us?” Without this clarity, AI initiatives risk becoming unfocused and ineffective. 🎯

Data: The Foundation of AI Success 📈

A recurring insight was the critical role of data in unlocking AI’s potential. Clean, structured, and interconnected data is essential for meaningful insights and automation. Many organizations have accumulated large volumes of data but lack the strategy to manage or leverage it effectively.

Brand emphasized that data quality—not just quantity—determines AI success. Poor data leads to inaccurate outputs, which can have serious business consequences, especially in areas like forecasting or supply chain planning. Establishing a robust data strategy is therefore a prerequisite for any AI-driven transformation. 🧠

Integrated Ecosystems Over Fragmented Solutions 🔗

When considering procurement transformation, organizations face a choice between best-of-breed tools and integrated solution suites. The podcast highlighted a growing trend toward integrated ecosystems, particularly in the age of AI.

Unified platforms provide consistent data models and seamless process integration, enabling more advanced analytics and automation. This holistic approach enhances visibility, reduces complexity, and supports enterprise-wide optimization. While niche solutions may address specific needs, they often lack the broader context required for strategic decision-making. 🏗️

Balancing Innovation with Realism ⚖️

A key takeaway was the importance of setting realistic expectations. While innovation is essential, organizations must recognize that enterprise-grade solutions evolve at a measured pace. Providers are balancing cutting-edge capabilities with reliability, scalability, and business-critical accuracy.

Transparent communication between vendors and customers is vital. By setting clear expectations, demonstrating current capabilities, and outlining a roadmap for future innovation, organizations can build trust and ensure alignment throughout the transformation journey. 🤝

The Future of Procurement: Evolution, Not Revolution 🔮

Looking ahead, Brand offered a pragmatic perspective on the future of procurement. While technology will continue to evolve, the core principles of procurement—relationship management, strategic sourcing, and value creation—will remain constant.

AI and automation will augment, not replace, human expertise. Procurement professionals will continue to play a critical role in decision-making, supplier relationships, and strategic planning. The future lies in blending advanced technology with human insight to drive sustainable value. 👥

Final Thoughts 💡

Procurement transformation is not a one-time initiative but an ongoing journey requiring strategic clarity, disciplined execution, and continuous improvement. Technology and AI are powerful enablers, but their success depends on foundational elements—clear goals, strong data, and organizational commitment.

As organizations navigate this evolving landscape, the winners will be those who balance ambition with realism, leveraging innovation while staying grounded in core business principles. The question is not whether to transform—but how thoughtfully and effectively that transformation is pursued. ✅

Listen on APPLE!

Listen on Spotify!

entry.title: SAP’s Autonomous Enterprise: beyond the announcement, toward the architecture
entry.content:
When SAP says it’s becoming a ‘Business AI company,’ that’s not marketing. It’s a structural admission that the ERP paradigm built around systems of record is being replaced by something that executes on your behalf.

Here’s what I find most interesting as a technical architect, and what I think gets underplayed.

The architecture

Most coverage focuses on Joule, the 224 agents, and the Claude partnership. These are components. What matters is how they stack.

Figure 1: SAP Joule Architecture Stack

SAP isn’t trying to build the world’s best AI model. It’s building an AI-powered enterprise operating system. An Anthropic partnership makes sense because the roles are distinct: Claude handles reasoning, and SAP handles domain intelligence and governance.

“For the mission-critical processes of our customers, ‘almost right’ just isn’t good enough.” Quote by Christian Klein, CEO, SAP

What the Autonomous Finance demo actually tells us

The most concrete example from Sapphire: a CFO asks Joule to prepare a bank briefing. Minutes later, a completed presentation surfaces with live data, flagged risks, and analysis. That used to take hours across multiple people.

That’s a testable claim, which is the right kind of proof point for enterprise buyers. The same logic applies across Autonomous Spend and Autonomous Supply Chain.

The Autonomous Close Assistant compresses the financial close from weeks to days by automating journal entries, reconciliation, and error resolution. In financial systems with regulatory requirements, there’s no tolerance for ‘good enough.’ That’s what makes the SAP Knowledge Graph central here. It’s not just retrieving data. It’s giving agents the business context to act correctly.

How SAP solved the governance problem

What makes this architecture different from previous enterprise AI attempts is the governed execution layer SAP built around the agents. Joule doesn’t connect directly to agents. Every request routes through the SAP Agent Gateway, which handles identity authentication, principal propagation, and policy enforcement before any agent acts. Agents can collaborate via Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol, but every A2A interaction is routed through that gateway. No agent acts outside the governed path. No identity is lost between hops. Every interaction is auditable.n enterprise software, that last point determines whether a CFO will actually sign off on autonomous finance processes.

Figure 2: SAP Joule — Joule as orchestrator, SAP Agent Gateway, A2A protocol, and end-to-end governed flow

The SAP and NVIDIA collaboration on Joule Studio runtime adds another layer. NVIDIA OpenShell provides isolated execution environments, filesystem and network policy enforcement, and runtime containment. SAP contributes the enterprise governance side: roles, identity lifecycle, policy semantics, observability. OpenShell answers ‘Can this agent action safely execute?’ Joule Studio runtime answers ‘Should this action happen at all?’ SAP is a key contributor to the OpenShell open source project. The Joule Studio Early Adopter Program is open now.

What this means for the SAP architect

The required skill set is shifting. The next generation of SAP architects won’t train foundation models, but they will need to understand how agentic systems fit into existing business process logic.

Figure 4: SAP Architect Skill Stack — core platform, agentic layer, AI fundamentals

Agent mining is one of the less-discussed announcements worth paying attention to. It’s an extension of process mining: it catalogs what agents did, where they slowed down, and whether they behaved as expected. The AI Agent Hub, included free for all SAP Business AI Platform customers, is where this governance lives. As more agents start operating inside finance and supply chain, this observability is not optional.

Functional expertise in finance, supply chain, and procurement doesn’t get less valuable here. Someone has to define what ‘correct’ looks like for an agent operating in those domains. The architect who can connect deep process knowledge with agentic design patterns will be at the center of these transformations.

My take

SAP Sapphire 2026 moved the enterprise AI conversation from roadmap to production intent. The partnerships with Anthropic, AWS, NVIDIA, and Google Cloud are real. The domain depth built from 50 years of ERP process logic is a genuine differentiator that general-purpose AI platforms will find hard to replicate.

Figure 3: SAP Autonomous Enterprise — Autonomous Suite, Joule Work, Business AI Platform and key partners

SAP has also been serious about the data foundation underneath the agents. The Dremio acquisition makes SAP Business Data Cloud an Apache Iceberg-native lakehouse for SAP and non-SAP data. Prior Labs brings tabular foundation modelling in-house. Reltio adds master data management. SAP-RPT-1.5 is a relational pretrained transformer built for structured enterprise data. The model is a commodity layer. The data and context SAP has assembled are not.

What SAP has built is the coordination layer between AI systems and the systems of record they need to operate inside. That’s been the missing piece in enterprise AI for two years. Joule Studio, the Agent Gateway, agent mining via the AI Agent Hub, and the NVIDIA OpenShell runtime together answer the question most agentic initiatives couldn’t: not whether an agent can act, but whether you can prove it was allowed to.

One question I keep sitting with: will enterprise AI be won by the best model, or by the platform that best combines business context, governance, and execution? I suspect it’s the latter. SAP is making a credible bet on exactly that.

#SAP  #SAPPHIRE2026  #AutonomousEnterprise  #EnterpriseAI  #Joule  #SAPBTP  #AgenticAI  #DigitalTransformation

entry.title: Conversational Analytics in SAP Joule with SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
In today’s digital landscape, intuitive insights and intelligent assistance are becoming essential for business users. SAP Joule, the AI-powered digital assistant from SAP, takes this a step further by enabling natural-language queries and actionable insights across enterprise applications. One of the most impactful capabilities of Joule is its ability to connect with SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) - bringing conversational analytics directly into business workflows.

This blog provides a simplified overview of how SAP Joule is integrated with SAP Analytics Cloud using SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP).

Prerequisites for Joule Onboarding

  • Entitlements for Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication and Identity Provisioning.
  • To support navigation to different applications from Joule and handle role-based access scenarios, you need to configure the navigation service of SAP Build Work Zone.

Note: By default, SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition uses the SAP Authentication and Trust Management service (XSUAA) for identity authentication. Therefore, we recommend first switching to SAP Cloud Identity Services - Identity Authentication as the authentication mechanism.

Required Applications/Services

  • Joule à Foundation
  • SAP Build Work Zone, Standard Edition à Foundation or Standard

Here are the steps for configuring Joule:

  1. Configure Destination to Use Navigation Service by creating a Navigation service destination
  2. Configure Trust to the Identity Authentication Tenant by adding the IAS URL in the joule subaccount trusted configuration
  3. Configure User Attributes for Joule from the Identity Directory
  4. Configure Trusted Domains in Identity Authentication
  5. Integration Process:

In the current release of Analytical Pattern in Joule, at least one existing Joule setup is required for any line of business (LoB), such as SAP SuccessFactors, SAP S/4HANA Cloud, or SAP Build Work Zone. Joule can also be used in stand-alone mode.

Here, we are using SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud System.

Since most SAP customers already have Joule activated, we address both configuration options below.

Option 1

  • Create a new SAP BTP Subaccount.
  • Run Joule booster

 

After completing the Joule Booster process, you will see the Joule formation in Ready status.

 

This completes the Joule activation for analytical insights. However, additional configuration steps are required.

Option 2

  • Within your SAP BTP Global Account, navigate to Formations, and search for the Joule formation you have already set up. You can click on Include System.

 

 

  • Review the included system. The system is now ready, which completes the Joule activation for analytical insights.

6. Post-integration checks

  • Once we follow the steps above, you can validate if the BTP Destination is created in your SAP BTP Subaccount. Destination named – “DYN_SAC_SSO”. Also, verify that the OAuth Client was created in your SAP Analytics Cloud system.

 

  • In your SAP Analytics Cloud, you should see a new line item for Joule in the SAC Roles. Ensure that the Read option is selected after the Joule Integration.

7. Create an OAuth Client for the Design Time in SAP Analytics Cloud

  • Log in to your SAP Analytics Cloud system and create OAuth client under the Configured Clients. 

 

 

  • Using this certificate, create a Trusted Identity Provider named Joule_IDP_Manual in SAC

8. Consume Role Definition Content from SAP Analytics Cloud

  • Log in to your SAP Analytics Cloud system and create an OAuth Client for Runtime in SAP Analytics Cloud.
  • Create an OAuth Client for Runtime in SAP Analytics Cloud
  • Note: Store the Secret value for DT and RT securely, as it will not be visible later.

9. Create a Design Time Destination in your SAP BTP Subaccount

After creating the destination, in additional Properties add the below properties.

HTML5.SetXForwardedHeaders: false &

URL.headers.x-sap-sac-custom-auth: true

10. Create a Runtime Destination by Cloning in our SAP BTP Subaccount

Select the existing DYN_SAC_SSO destination from BTP Joule subaccount and choose Duplicate to create a new destination based of it named (SAC_SWZ_RT)

11. Create a Content Provider in SAP Build Work Zone

To create a content provider in the SAP Build Work Zone, ensure you have the "Launchpad_Admin" role assigned.

 

12. Provision Users from SAP Analytics Cloud to SAP Build Work Zone Using IPS

Note: This process provisions users from SAP Analytics Cloud to SAP Build Work Zone. You will be working in SAP Cloud Identity Services and will need Administrator access.

 

  • Setup SAP SAC as a Source System in IAS
  • Setup SAP Build Work Zone as a Target System in IAS

 

 

13. Run User and Group Provisioning Job in IAS

              Check provisioning Logs to see the status.

14. Validate Provisioning

  • Open Build work zone application from BTP
  • Go to Channel Manager and refresh it to validate the provisioning.

15. Index Your Data Using Just Ask and Test Your Integration

The data available for your business users to ask Joule analytical questions is the data from supported models indexed by the just ask feature in your SAP Analytics Cloud tenant. Analytical insights support the following indexed models:

  • Models acquired from external sources and imported into SAP Analytics Cloud.
  • Analytic models from SAP Datasphere that are part of an SAP Business Data Cloud formation that also includes your SAP Analytics Cloud system.

Your users will need different permissions in SAP Analytics Cloud depending on their role:

  • To index models in the just ask feature of SAP Analytics Cloud, users will need the Maintain permission for the Just Ask privilege.
  • To ask questions to Joule's analytical insights feature and view answers and charts, users need the Read permission for the Joule privilege.

16. Open your S/4HANA Public Cloud instance and launch Joule

Query: - Give me a chart of discount per product Coca-Cola and per store

Conclusion: -

The integration of SAP Joule with SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) represents a significant advancement in making business intelligence more accessible through natural language interactions. By leveraging SAP Business Technology Platform, SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Cloud Identity Services, and SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, organizations can enable conversational analytics that empowers users to explore data, generate insights, and visualize results without requiring deep technical expertise.

Successful implementation requires careful configuration of authentication, trust relationships, destinations, OAuth clients, content providers, and user provisioning. Once integrated, Joule can seamlessly access indexed SAC models through the Just Ask capability, allowing users to retrieve analytical insights, charts, and business information through simple conversational queries.

This integration enhances user productivity, accelerates decision-making, and promotes self-service analytics by bringing AI-driven insights directly into daily business workflows. As organizations continue their digital transformation journey, SAP Joule and SAP Analytics Cloud together provide a powerful foundation for intelligent, data-driven enterprise operations.

entry.title: Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part1
entry.content:
SAP Joule adoption is booming across SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP SuccessFactors, SAP Digital Supply Chain, SAP Ariba, SAP Concur, and beyond. As more teams activate Joule, configuration and integration challenges naturally emerge — whether you're just getting started or optimizing an existing setup. 

We created this guide to make troubleshooting easier. Based on real-world experience with Joule customers, it offers a straightforward, step-by-step approach to help administrators quickly identify and resolve issues. You'll find practical solutions whether you're tackling an activation challenge or addressing a business user concern. 

Our aim is simple: help you solve problems faster and work more effectively with SAP Support when you need it. Let's get started.  

This blog is part of the Joule Activation series: 

  • SAP Business AI – Overview for all!!!
  • Joule - Unified Setup: Bridging Simplicity and Performance
  • Joule for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition - A Comprehensive Setup Guide
  • Joule – End-to-End Setup Guide (for all Line of Business) Unified Approach
  • Managing Joule on SAP BTP: Roles, Analytics, Administration and Troubleshooting
  • SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition and Joule health checks
  • Common configuration issues: Joule for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 
  • SAP Joule – Common Configuration Issues 

This blog is based on supporting customer managed Joule implementations, and this blog is part1. The below series of the blogs are core RIG content on Joule Administration and we plan on keeping them up to date (such as with relevant information for SAP Managed Joule implementations in future),  which includes:

  • Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part1
  • Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part2
  • Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part3
  • Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part4

Understanding the Joule Architecture for Issue Resolution Layers

This diagram shows the simplified runtime workflow for SAP Joule, illustrating how users interact with SAP Build Work Zone on SAP BTP and connect to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition through Cloud Connector, with authentication handled by SAP Cloud Identity Services. 

Before diving into troubleshooting, it is essential to understand how Joule operates across multiple architectural layers, let me make it vertical for better understanding. Each layer is a potential critical touchpoint and understanding the stack helps isolate issues quickly. 

How Joule processes a request at runtime: 

  1. The user interacts with the Joule client embedded in an SAP application (Layer 1).
  2. Joule authenticates via SAP Cloud Identity Services (Layer 2) and receives an OpenID Connect (OIDC) token. 
  3. Joule queries the SAP Build Work Zone Navigation Service (Layer 3) to determine which capabilities the user has access to, based on role-content mappings. 
  4. Joule's orchestration evaluates the user prompt against the Scenario Catalog, Knowledge Catalog, and user context (Layer 4). 
  5. If a transactional/navigational capability is matched, Joule calls the backend system via SAP BTP Destination Service and SAP Cloud Connector (Layer 5) using Principal Propagation. 
  6. The backend (Layer 6) processes the request and returns data based on the user's authorizations. 
  7. Joule returns the response to the user. 

Understanding this flow is the key to effective troubleshooting.

We are moving on part2 for the issue resolution workflow.

entry.title: Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part2
entry.content:
The Recommended Issue Resolution Workflow

This blog is part2. The below series of the blogs are core RIG content on Joule Administration and we plan on keeping them up to date (such as with relevant information for SAP Managed Joule implementations in future),  which includes:

  • Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part1
  • Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part2
  • Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part3
  • Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part4

The following workflow provides a systematic approach to resolving Joule issues. It is divided into two phases: Initial triage (performed by the user or first-level support) and Diagnostic Investigation (performed by the Joule administrator).

Phase 1: Initial Triage

Step 1: Identify the Symptom

Before diving into troubleshooting, take a moment to classify the issue. The fastest path to a fix starts with asking: what kind of problem is it? Match the reported symptom to one of these common categories:

Symptom (Most Common)

Likely Category

Joule icon not visible

UX / Icon Issue

Blank/white screen after clicking icon

SAP Cloud Identity Services - Identity Authentication (IAS) / Identity Provisioning (IPS)

"Refused to connect" error

Trusted Domains / Connectivity

"Digital Assistant not found"

Formation / SAP BTP Provisioning

Joule only provides SAP Help documentation

Content / Roles / Capabilities

"I'm having trouble connecting"

Connectivity / Principal Propagation

Joule not greeting with user's name

Identity / Global User ID

Booster execution fails

Joule Booster / Activation

Step 2: Download Traces

A quick but important step before escalating any Joule issue: collect your diagnostic data upfront. Fortunately, Joule makes this straightforward. Head to Settings > About > Download Traces, decide whether to include message history, and save the .zip file. This single package contains a wealth of troubleshooting information, including: Joule version and environment details, UI logs and recent HTTP requests, Correlation IDs for the last 5-10 messages, Message history (if consented).

Step 3: Determine the Response Type and Classify the Issue Layer

Per SAP Note 3679363 How to recognize a Joule response's type?, Joule has three response types:

Response Type

How to Recognize

Meaning

Direct Response

Generic content, exclamation mark on hover (Response Insights)

Joule could not match any capability or knowledge source

Information Retrieval

Response includes SAP Help documentation links or custom document sources

Joule matched knowledge content but not a transactional capability

Transactional / Navigational

Response returns business data or app navigation buttons

Capability was successfully selected and executed

A mismatch between the expected and actual response type is a telltale sign of a deeper issue. If you're expecting a transactional or navigational response but Joule returns an informational or direct answer instead, this points to a content, role, or configuration problem that warrants a closer look by your administrator.

Tips:

Joule Response Types (SAP Note 3679363 How to recognize a Joule response's type?)

Direct Response: Generic "I cannot..." + exclamation icon on hover Information Retrieval: Response includes SAP Help doc links Transactional/Navigational: Returns business data or app button

If expected Transactional but got Informational/Direct: -> Follow Path D below (Content/Roles)

The fastest way to get to the bottom of a Joule issue is to match the symptom to the right architecture layer. Use the table from the github repository ([Github] leee03/SAP_Joule_Issue_Catalog) as your starting point — it maps common symptoms to the layer most likely responsible, so you know exactly where to focus your investigation, a simplified view as below:

Symptom

Diagnostic Path

Architecture Layer

Joule icon not visible in SAP Fiori Launchpad

Path F: UX / Icon Appearance

Layer 1: Client/UX

"Refused to connect" / "I'm having trouble connecting"

Path E: Connectivity / Principal Propagation

Layer 5: Connectivity

Joule returns SAP Help content / generic instructions only

Path D : Content Exposure & Roles

Layer 3: SAP Build Work Zone + Layer 6: Backend

"Digital Assistant not found"

 Path E: Connectivity / (Formation)

 Layer 5: Connectivity

Joule not greeting with user name / blank screen

Path C: SAP Cloud Identity Services

Layer 2: SAP Cloud Identity Services

Booster execution fails / formation errors

Path B: Joule Booster / Activation

Layer 4: Joule Service

Entitlements missing / systems not in landscape

Path A: SAP BTP Provisioning

Layer 7: SAP Business Technology Platform

Content Provider sync fails / unauthorized

Path D : Content Exposure & Roles

Layer 3: SAP Build Work Zone

The diagnostic path detailed will be mentioned in below chapter in part3.

Let us continue to part 3!

entry.title: Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part3
entry.content:
This blog is part3. The below series of the blogs are core RIG content on Joule Administration and we plan on keeping them up to date (such as with relevant information for SAP Managed Joule implementations in future),  which includes:

  • Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part1
  • Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part2
  • Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part3
  • Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part4

Phase 2: Diagnostic Investigation (Joule Administrator)

Based on the symptom category identified in Phase 1, follow the appropriate diagnostic path below.

Step 4a: Apply fixes if you would like self-resolvable.

  • Follow the corresponding Path (A-F) below in sequence, please check the details steps for each path;
  • Each step builds on the previous — do not skip steps

Step 5: Verify Fixes

  • If resolved, verify with a transactional prompt (e.g. "Display Sales Order 911");
  • Run health checks;
  • If not resolved after all steps, open SAP Support ticket with Correlation ID and traces together with step 4b to collect diagnostics

Step 4b: Collect Diagnostics with below necessary information

  • Correlation ID from traces
  • SAP Cloud Connector logs
  • SAP BTP Destination test
  • IPS job status

Step 6: Open SAP Support ticket with below necessary information

  • Use correct component
  • Attach traces & correlation ID
  • Include system version & steps

Diagnostic Paths

Path A: SAP BTP Provisioning Issues

This path resolves: missing Joule entitlements; missing systems in the visible landscape; and subaccount creation issues.

Step

Action

What to Check

A1

Verify Joule entitlements

SAP BTP Global Account > Entitlements > Service Assignments > Search "Joule"

A2

Verify system registration

SAP BTP Global Account > System Landscape > Systems

A3

Check data center compatibility

Data Centers Supported by Joule

A4

Validate subaccount configuration

Ensure Cloud Foundry (CF) enabled, correct region selected

Key SAP Notes:

  • 3639572 - Enabling Joule with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 
  • 3456718 - Menu "System Landscape" not visible 
  • 3661817 - SAP systems missing in System Landscape 

Path B: Joule Booster / Activation Issues

This path resolves: booster execution failures; missing formation prerequisites; and subscription errors.

Step

Action

What to Check

B1

Verify IAS tenant visibility

SAP BTP Global Account > System Landscape > Systems (IAS should be listed)

B2

Check IAS-Global Account linkage

Ensure IAS tenant matches SAP BTP Global Account's customer ID

B3

Verify SAP Build Work Zone subscription

Must be a subscription (not just an instance) in the Joule subaccount

B4

Trigger system sync

Click "Refresh" button in System Landscape if systems are missing

B5

Check formation type

Ensure correct capabilities selected in booster Step 4

Common errors and fixes:

SAP BTP Error Message

Fix

"Your global account does not have an Identity Authentication service tenant"

Configure trust between IAS and Joule subaccount.

SAP Note 3675105 - Joule Setup Error: "Your global account does not have an identity authentication service tenant"

"Could not find SAP Cloud Identity system with host..."

Ensure IAS linked to correct SAP BTP Global Account. Wait up to 1 week for sync.

SAP Note 3620409 - Could not find SAP Cloud Identity system with host: Joule booster error

"SUBSCRIBE_FAILED" for das-application

Unsubscribe and retry. If persistent, raise incident CA-JOULE-PRV-ONB.

SAP Note 3689452 - Entity Saas Subscription failed after running Joule Booster

Work Zone not in dropdown (Step 5)

Add SAP Build Work Zone subscription (not instance).

SAP Note 3710864 SAP Build Work Zone system not visible in dropdown during setup

SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Option not in Step 3 dropdown

Select in Step 4 "Select Capabilities" instead. SAP Note 3710921 - S/4HANA Private Cloud option is not listed in Step 3 - "Select Integration" in Joule Booster

Path C: SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS/IPS) Issues

This path resolves: single sign-on (SSO) issues; blank/empty login screens; mismatched user identities; and IPS synchronization failures.

Step

Action

What to Check

C1

Verify IAS trust domain

SAP BTP Joule Subaccount > Trust Configuration > Domain field must match SAP product domain

C2

Check IAS is used as proxy

Fiori Launchpad in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition must authenticate via IAS (not directly to corporate IDP)

C3

Validate Global User ID

Transaction SU01 > User > Goto > External User ID must match IAS Identity Directory Global User ID

C4

Check domain consistency

All systems must use same top-level domain (*.cloud.sap or *.ondemand.com)

C5

Verify IPS jobs

SAP Cloud Identity Services > Identity Provisioning > check job status and errors

C6

Validate transformations

Ensure IPS source/target transformations use latest version from SAP Help

Key indicators:

  • Joule not greeting with user's name → Global User ID mapping issue (Step C3)
  • Blank/white screen or IAS login prompt → Domain mismatch (Step C1). See  SAP Note 3635949 Blank white / IAS login screen appears when clicking on Joule icon from the solutions where Joule was integrated into

  •  

    "Corp IDP refused to connect" → IAS not configured as proxy for corporate Identity Provider (Step C2)

Path D：Content Exposure & Role Issues

This path resolves: wrong Joule response type (informational/direct instead of transactional/navigational); and missing Joule capabilities.

This is the most common category of issues encountered in production. Follow these steps sequentially:

Step

Action

What to Check

D1

Verify capability availability

Joule in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition capabilities

D2

Check system version support

SAP 3523238 - Navigational and Transactional capabilities with Joule in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition attachment, column F (minimum S/4HANA version per capability)

D3

Identify required Semantic Objects & Actions

SAP Note 3523238 attachment spreadsheet

D4

Verify business role contains required catalogs

Transaction PFCG > Role > Menu tab > Check business catalogs contain required Fiori App IDs

D5

Check content exposure

Transaction /UI2/CDM3_EXP_SCOPE > Verify role is exposed (V1 or V2)

D6

Verify CDM content readable

Test CDM URL directly: /sap/bc/http/ui2/flp_content_exposure/entities (V2) or /sap/bc/ui2/cdm3/entities (V1)

D7

Refresh Channel Manager

SAP Build Work Zone Admin > Channel Manager > Update/Refresh

D8

Verify apps in Content Manager

SAP Build Work Zone Admin > Content Manager > Find role > Check Semantic Objects & Actions

D9

Check user role assignment in SAP Build Work Zone

SAP Build Work Zone Admin > Settings > Identity Provisioning tab > Search user > Verify roles listed

D10

Verify OData services activated

Check APIs listed in SAP Note 3523238 are active (transaction SICF or /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE)

Important notes:

  • When using custom business roles, make sure they mimic the content of SAP business roles. They must reference the same Launchpad Application Descriptor Items (LADIs) (aka tile/target mapping definitions) as the original SAP business role.For Master/Derived Role models, expose the Master Role only (derived roles cannot be exposed independently).
  • After a system upgrade, you must refresh Joule's capability catalog by excluding and re-including the system in the Joule formation.
  • Schedule a regular content update job to keep CDM content synchronized.
  • See what to check in these SAP Learning Videos: "Resolving Missing Business Roles for Joule in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition" and "Resolving Inactive ICF and OData Services for Joule in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition".

Path E: Connectivity & Principal Propagation Issues

This path resolves: "I'm having trouble connecting" errors; "backend not reachable" errors; and timeout errors.

Step

Action

What to Check

E1

Test Principal Propagation

Copy S4HANA_PCE_SSO destination as "authtest", add HTML5.DynamicDestination=true, test CDM URL through SAP Build Work Zone

E2

Check SAP Cloud Connector

Verify system mapping, certificate validity, principal type = "X.509 certificate"

E3

Enable SAP Cloud Connector logs

Turn on tunnel trace and TLS trace for detailed error information

E4

Check SAP BTP destinations

Verify S4HANA_PCE_SSO destination configuration and connection test

E5

Verify trusted domains

Add SAP Fiori Launchpad URL to IAS Trusted Domains AND SAP BTP Security > Settings

E6

Trace in backend

Transaction /n/IWFND/TRACES for HTTP payload trace; SMICM for HTTP trace level 1

E7

Check CERTRULE

Transaction CERTRULE for rule-based certificate mappings

Troubleshooting resources:

  • How to troubleshoot SAP Cloud Connector Principal Propagation over HTTPS
  • Pro tip: Enable SAP Cloud Connector Alerting in production to receive email notifications 30 days before certificates expire. This prevents connectivity outages proactively.

Path F: UX / Icon Appearance Issues

This path resolves: Joule icon not visible; Joule icon displayed incorrectly; and Joule icon is visible but does not launch the Joule dialog.

Step

Action

What to Check

F1

Check 3rd party cookies

Ensure browser allows 3rd party cookies for the Joule/IAS domains

F2

Verify SAPUI5 version

Must be equal to or higher than minimum supported version. 

See SAP Note 3641945 - Joule icon displayed wrongly in Fiori launchpad of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition

F3

Check user role for Joule plug-in

Ensure user has a single role containing the SAP Fiori business catalogue for Joule

F4

Clear caches

Browser cache + server cache via program /UI2/INVALIDATE_GLOBAL_CACHES

F5

Check SAP Fiori customizing conflicts

Transaction /UI2/FLC - check "Configuration" first, then "Customizing"

F6

Verify trusted domains

Add URLs to IAS Tenant Settings > Trusted Domains AND SAP BTP subaccount > Security > Settings

Continued with part4 on the issue summary and key takeways.

entry.title: Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part4
entry.content:
this blog is part4. The below series of the blogs are core RIG content on Joule Administration and we plan on keeping them up to date (such as with relevant information for SAP Managed Joule implementations in future),  which includes:

  • Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part1
  • Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part2
  • Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part3
  • Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part4

Issue Catalog Summary

In this github, you’ll find a consolidated catalog of documented Joule issues, organized by architecture layer to make navigation as intuitive as possible. For the full picture — including detailed root causes, resolutions, SAP Notes, support components - just git and download the attached Excel file. And we’d love to keep this resource growing: if you have additional issues to contribute, you’re warmly invited to add the valid ones.

[Github] leee03/SAP_Joule_Issue_Catalog

Layer 1: Client / UX

  • Frame-ancestors Content Security Policy (CSP) violation
  • Joule icon not showing in SAP Fiori Launchpad
  • Icon displayed incorrectly (SAPUI5 version)
  • Unable to launch Joule via icon (cookies)
  • Icon not appearing for specific user (SAP SuccessFactors)
  • Blank/greyed-out screen (SAP SuccessFactors)

Layer 2: SAP Cloud Identity Services

  • Corporate Identity Provider refused to connect
  • Joule not greeting with user's name
  • Blank/white IAS login screen
  • IPS transformation editing fails
  • IAS source system read job failing

Layer 3: SAP Build Work Zone

  • Content Manager not visible (foundation plan limitation)
  • Content provider sync fails (unauthorized)
  • Navigation links not working (proxy type)
  • Capabilities limited with foundation vs standard plan
  • Joule cannot return transactional/navigational responses
  • SuccessFactors navigational use cases not working - profile button missing, deep links not generated

Layer 4: Joule Service

  • IAS tenant not found during booster prerequisites
  • Cloud Identity system host not found
  • IAS system missing from System Landscape
  • Build Work Zone not in booster dropdown
  • SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Option missing from booster
  • SaaS subscription failed (das-application)
  • Formation creation error (IAS not enabled in Work Zone)
  • Joule scenario selected but execution fails with empty response/error/timeout

Layer 5: Connectivity

  • "I'm having trouble connecting" error
  • SAP Cloud Connector cannot connect to subaccount
  • "Digital Assistant not found" error
  • Runtime destination proxy type incorrectly set causing navigation link failures

Layer 6: Backend System

  • Required OData services not activated
  • Business roles missing required semantic objects
  • Content exposure not configured correctly
  • Custom roles not working with Joule - missing semantic objects, App IDs, or wrong CDM exposure

Layer 7: SAP Business Technology Platform

  • Joule service not available in subaccount entitlement
  • Entitlements not available despite supported data center
  • System Landscape menu not visible
  • SAP systems not visible in SAP BTP System Landscape
  • E2E setup requires high manual effort due to documentation gaps

SAP Support Components Reference

When self-resolution is not possible, raise an SAP incident using the appropriate component:

Component

Scope

CA-JOULE

General Joule service issues and functionality

CA-JOULE-PRV-ONB

Joule Subscription, Booster, Provisioning errors

CA-JOULE-PRV / CA-JOULE-PRV-INT

Provisioning and integrations with applications

CA-FLP-EXT-PCE-JOULE

Joule integration with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition

CA-JOULE-CLT

Joule Messaging Client related topics

CA-JOULE-CLT-COP

Joule x Microsoft Copilot integration

CA-JOULE-CR

Joule Conversational Runtime

CA-JOULE-CI

Joule Conversation Logging and Insights

CA-JOULE-TA

Tenant Administration / Content Deployment

CA-JOULE-TR

Tenant Runtime (Bot-runtime), Approuter issues

BC-CP-MP-FOR

Unified Customer Landscape (UCL) / Formations

BC-IAM-IDS

SAP Cloud Identity Services - Identity Authentication (IAS)

BC-IAM-IPS

SAP Cloud Identity Services - Identity Provisioning (IPS)

EP-WZ-ONB / EP-WZ-STA

SAP Build Work Zone

LOD-SF-PLT-JOU

Joule in SAP SuccessFactors

 BC-MID-SCC

SAP Cloud Connector

When raising a ticket, always include:

  • SAP S/4HANA system name and release version
  • Correlation ID (from Joule traces)
  • Downloaded trace .zip file
  • Screenshots of the error
  • Steps to reproduce

See SAP Note 3666014 - Information required for Joule troubleshooting for the complete list of information SAP Support requires.

Troubleshooting Toolkit: Verify Your Configuration

Here is the troubleshooting toolkit organized by architecture layer:

Layer 1: Client / UX

  • Joule Download Traces (Settings > About)
  • Correlation ID (last 5-10 messages in trace)
  • Transaction /UI2/FLC — SAP Fiori Launchpad content check
  • Transaction /UI2/INVALIDATE_GLOBAL_CACHES — Cache clear

Layer 2: SAP Cloud Identity Services

  • IAS Admin Console (authentication logs, trust configuration)
  • IPS Admin Console (provisioning job status, transformation errors)

Layer 3: SAP Build Work Zone

  • SAP Build Work Zone Navigation API

Layer 4: Joule Service

  • Joule Booster logs (execution history, error details)
  • Formation configuration status in SAP BTP Cockpit

Layer 5: Connectivity

  • Transaction SMICM — ICM trace (Principal Propagation)
  • SAP Cloud Connector Logs (tunnel/TLS trace)
  • SAP BTP Destination — Connection Test

Layer 6: Backend System

  • Transaction /n/IWFND/TRACES — HTTP Payload trace
  • Transaction /UI2/CDM3_EXP_SCOPE — Content Exposure

Layer 7: SAP Business Technology Platform

  • SAP BTP Cockpit UI (entitlements, system landscape, subaccount config)

Health Checks: Validate Your Configuration

Before onboarding business users, perform these quick validation tests or dobule checks after apply fixes:

  • Test a transactional capability: Prompt Joule with "Display Sales Order 911" (or a valid order number). If Joule returns actual business data, the end-to-end configuration is working.
  • Test Principal Propagation: Use the CDM URL test through SAP Build Work Zone as described in Path E above.
  • Test content exposure: Access the CDM URL directly in a browser with the technical user (V1: http://<server>:<port>/sap/bc/ui2/cdm3/entities or V2: http://<server>:<port>/sap/bc/http/ui2/flp_content_exposure/entities)
  • Verify user roles in SAP Build Work Zone: SAP Build Work Zone Admin > Settings > Identity Provisioning > Search for user > Confirm roles are listed.
  • Check Navigation API: Access the navigation API to verify intent-based navigation targets.
  • All the details in SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition and Joule health checks

Key Takeaways

Start Here: Identify the Problem Layer

Before fixing anything, figure out where the issue lives. This saves time and prevents fixing the wrong thing.

Most Common Problem

Joule gives information instead of completing tasks =  This is almost always a role/content exposure/IPS sync issue (Path D).

Quick Checklist (Check These First)

  •  Are user roles synced to SAP Build Work Zone?
  •  Is the content accessible/exposed?
  •  Is Principal Propagation working?

Prevention

  • Turn on Cloud Connector alerts
  • Schedule regular content sync jobs
  • Refresh systems after updates

Before Contacting SAP Support

  1. Download Joule traces
  2. Save the Correlation ID
  3. Send both with your ticket

In short: Troubleshoot systematically, check permissions first, monitor regularly, and collect error details before reaching out for support.

Additional Resources

  • SAP Help: Joule Portal
  • SAP Help: Joule Constraints
  • SAP Help: Monitoring and Troubleshooting
  • Reference Architecture: Identity and Access Management for Joule
  • 3679305 - Joule configuration checking when it cannot return the response of transactional or navigational capability prompts
  • Joule Roadmap

Acknowledgments

This blog consolidates insights from the SAP S/4HANA Customer Care and Regional Implementation Group, the AI RIG team, and the Joule product teams. Special thanks to colleagues and the broader SAP community who continue to share their experiences.

We will update this blog as new issues and resolutions are identified. If you have questions or feedback, please leave a comment below.

Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA Customer Care and RIG team

entry.title: Session 2 Recap: What SAP's Evolved Portfolio means for Architects
entry.content:
SAP’s evolved portfolio is more than a set of new AI features - it gives architects the context, data foundations, and governance needed to make AI work in the enterprise. This webinar makes one thing clear: the next wave of enterprise AI will belong to those who can connect intelligence to real business operations.

On 10 June 2026, the second Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise webinar brought together Mohamed Abdel Hadi, Habeebuddin Mohammed, Fabio Westphal, and Paul Kurchina for a strategic discussion on What SAP’s Evolved Portfolio means for Architects and the role of SAP MaxSuccess service offerings.

Register here to get access to the slides recordings and join the following sessions: https://events.sap.com/eaa-autent-web/en_us/home.html

 

Why AI stalls in the enterprise

A recurring theme throughout the webinar was that many AI initiatives fail for the same reasons. They are deployed on top of fragmented systems, disconnected data, and limited understanding of the business processes they are meant to support. That creates a familiar pattern: promising pilots that never scale.

The session made a strong case that the issue is not the intelligence of the model itself. The real challenge is whether the enterprise has built the context, integration, and governance layers needed for AI to operate effectively. Without those foundations, AI remains disconnected from the realities of the business.

Context is what makes AI useful

One of the strongest messages from the webinar was that AI needs context, not just intelligence. In an enterprise setting, AI cannot interpret data in isolation. It needs to understand business processes, relationships, and semantic meaning if it is going to support decisions and execution in a meaningful way.

That is where SAP’s portfolio evolution becomes important. The direction is clearly toward AI that is grounded in how the business actually runs. By linking intelligence to process knowledge and trusted enterprise data, SAP is shaping a model where AI is more relevant, more reliable, and far more useful in practice.

 

Integration is the real scaling challenge

The webinar also emphasized that data integration is not a secondary issue. It is one of the main barriers to scaling AI across the enterprise. When data is spread across systems and domains without a coherent architecture, AI cannot reason reliably across the business.

For architects, this is a critical reminder. Interoperability, trusted data foundations, and connected SAP and non-SAP landscapes are not implementation details. They are prerequisites for enterprise AI that can scale beyond isolated use cases.

Governance is what makes AI production-ready

Another major point from the session was the importance of governance. AI may look impressive in a demo or pilot, but it does not become enterprise-ready until it can be secured, audited, controlled, and managed throughout its lifecycle.

This is where the webinar shifted from technology capability to enterprise value. Governance is not a constraint on innovation. It is what allows organizations to innovate safely at scale. Enterprises that want to move beyond experimentation need the confidence to operationalize AI responsibly.

SAP’s Autonomous Enterprise vision

The webinar placed these ideas within SAP’s broader Autonomous Enterprise vision. In this model, AI is not treated as a separate layer added on top of the business. It becomes part of how the business runs.

That is an important distinction. It suggests a future where applications, workflows, data, and intelligent agents are designed to work together as a connected operating model. For architects, this means AI should be considered part of the enterprise architecture itself, not an add-on capability.

 

What architects should focus on

The architectural implication of the webinar is clear. The challenge is no longer just selecting AI tools. It is designing the foundation that allows AI to act intelligently and safely within the enterprise.

Three priorities stand out:

  • Business context, so AI understands how the enterprise operates.

  • Connected data, so AI can reason across systems and domains.

  • Governance, so AI can be trusted in production environments.

These are not separate workstreams. They are the core building blocks of contextual intelligence.

A modular AI future

The later part of the webinar pointed toward a more modular future for SAP’s AI portfolio. Capabilities such as Joule, Joule Studio, AI gateway patterns, and agent-based experiences suggest a move toward reusable building blocks that can be applied across multiple business scenarios.

This matters because modularity creates scale. Instead of building one-off AI solutions, organizations can create shared capabilities that support many use cases over time. That is a more sustainable model for enterprise adoption and one that aligns better with how architects think about platforms.

 

Platform thinking will matter more

A related message from the closing section was the need to think in terms of platforms rather than isolated solutions. The long-term value of AI will not come from one use case at a time. It will come from reusable foundations that can support multiple outcomes across the enterprise.

This is where architecture becomes strategic. Data, governance, orchestration, and integration need to be treated as enterprise assets. Organizations that take this platform approach will be better positioned to scale AI in a consistent and controlled way.

Why Reltio and Prior Labs matter

The discussion of Reltio and Prior Labs reinforced the same message. Reltio was positioned as a way to strengthen trusted business context, while Prior Labs reflected the importance of structured intelligence applied to enterprise data.

Together, they highlight a simple but powerful principle: enterprise AI needs both context and intelligence. Trusted data without intelligence is limited. Intelligence without trusted context is risky. The best outcomes come when both are brought together in a governed architecture.

 

Final takeaway

The central message from SAP’s webinar is that enterprise AI is not just a model challenge - it is an architectural one.

SAP’s evolved portfolio shows a clear move toward AI that is practical, contextual, and governed. For architects and technology leaders, the lesson is simple: success will depend less on the novelty of the AI itself and more on the strength of the foundations beneath it.

Business context, integration, governance, modularity, and platform thinking are becoming the real differentiators in the shift from AI experimentation to AI execution.

 

 

 

What Comes Next

  • The in-person tour: In addition to the webinar series, a six-city in-person learning tour is running across North America (Palo Alto, Atlanta, Dallas, Toronto, Newtown Square, and Calgary) bringing architects together face-to-face to discuss what they are learning through the series. Your can register for the In-Person Experience here: https://events.sap.com/eaa-aet/en_us/home.html

 

  • The series will adapt based on participant input. Surveys follow each session, and new topics or deeper dives will be added based on what architects tell the team they need most. You can still provide feedback here if you have not done already: Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise Webinar Series – Fill out form

 

  • Webinar# 3 - Architecting From Intent to Action with Joule Work & Knowledge Graph

The next session on 16 June will focus on From Intent to Action With Joule Work, and Knowledge Graph together with Gaurish Dessai, Jason Taylor and John Santic. Thank you to everyone who joined and hope you continue to support us through this webinar series!

entry.title: SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities including agentic capabilities is now available!
entry.content:
The re-invented SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities including agentic capabilities is now offered as a service across SAP BTP ABAP Environment, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition for releases 2021 and higher.

Joule speaks ABAP, and now it speaks more than ever before.

SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities have already generated strong interest across the SAP ecosystem, enabling customers to achieve remarkable results, as demonstrated by the success story from Bosch.

Following our Sapphire announcements, detailed in Sonja Lienard's blog post Sapphire 2026: GA announcement for agentic ABAP AI and in the blog post Sapphire 2026: ABAP AI - Chapter 3: We go agentic!  by Alexander Rother, we are now entering the agentic era of ABAP development.

In addition, we have received valuable feedback from many of you on the existing ABAP AI capabilities. Building on this feedback, we are proud to announce that our re-invented ABAP AI capabilities, including new agentic capabilities, are now officially available.

Beyond these enhancements, we have also expanded support for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, extending the supported releases down to 2021.

Please note : For SAP S/4HANA On-Premise 2025, 2023, 2022, and 2021, availability is subject to the special RISE with SAP contract conditions announced at SAP Sapphire. Please verify your entitlement and contract status with your account executive before planning adoption, refer to SAP Note 3521187 .

In this blog, we will cover the following aspects:

  1. How to obtain the required commercial license for SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities.
  2. How to configure your backend systems and enable SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities in ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse and ABAP Development Tools for Visual Studio Code including an overview of the capabilities supported

New Commercial model for SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI Capabilities

The introduction of the new ABAP AI Hub Service , which exposes our re-invented ABAP AI capabilities running side by side to your ABAP systems, marks a strategic step toward making AI capabilities more accessible, scalable, and future-ready across diverse SAP landscapes. With a single commercial license, customers can purchase once and leverage these capabilities across supported SAP products, independent of their underlying release versions.

One of the key advantages is simplified adoption. Customers can now enable ABAP AI capabilities through a more streamlined onboarding process using SAP for Me reducing setup effort while ensuring consistent lifecycle management across environments.

Another major benefit is release-independent access. Instead of being tightly coupled to specific SAP S/4HANA release cycles, customers can consume ABAP AI services side by side with their existing systems. This enables even customers on lower releases to leverage the latest AI innovations without waiting for a system upgrade.

In short, the ABAP AI Hub service is not just an architecture shift, it is an innovation enabler, helping customers adopt ABAP AI faster, more flexibly, and with greater future readiness.

Commercial model & AI Units

SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities are embedded in the Joule Premium package and offered with separate licenses for customers and partners. Pricing follows a consumption-based model, meaning you only pay for actual usage. Customers and partners purchase AI Units and activate the required packages via SAP for Me. Authorization for developer access is managed through Cloud Identity Services, while AI requests are executed and metered in the ABAP AI Hub Service on SAP BTP. 

What is an SAP AI Unit?

SAP AI Units are a form of virtual currency that customers purchase to activate and use premium AI capabilities within SAP Cloud Services. They are consumption-based credits that can be used flexibly across multiple SAP solutions and are not tied to a specific product or line of business. This allows customers to draw from a central pool of AI Units as they use AI-powered features across their SAP landscape. AI Units are purchased annually and expire after 12 months if not used. Customers can track their usage and remaining balance in real time via SAP for Me.

How do I know how many units I should purchase?

SAP provides guidance and estimation tools to help determine the right amount of AI Units. Your SAP Account Executive or Partner can support you in defining the appropriate quantity and help model different usage scenarios. 

How to Purchase the License and activate the packages? 

For Customers
Purchase AI Units using SKU 8019164 via SAP for Me to begin the Premium AI activation process.

For partners
Place the order for cloud test, demo, and development for SAP AI Unit (8019670). 

For more Information refer to the SAP 3740750 - SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities enhanced by agentic AI features - commercial license - SAP for Me

For Administrators: Steps to configure SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI Capabilities.

Prerequisites

You are an administrator with full access to SAP for Me.

Procedure

  • Open SAP for Me and navigate to Portfolio & Products.

  • Select the tab My Product Packages and choose the package Joule.


  • Select the tab Applications and choose the application SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities.
  • Now you can Start Provisioning.
    • Enter the solution details. Provide a display name and select a resource group. If no resource group exists, you will be prompted to create one.
    • Select a Cloud Identity Services system.
    • Select your region.

    • Choose a Formation Name (the name is mandatory but has no further technical relevance).


    • Review and finish the setup. The provision process might take a few moments to complete. Click on View in Resources to view the status of the generated resources.
    • The new solution will achieve the Ready status when all tenants are successfully provisioned, and integrations are fully established.

  • To activate the feature in SAP Central Business Configuration. Navigate to SAP for Me> Click on Portfolio & Provisioning  > Business AI.
  • Click on Configure to launch the Central Business Configuration application.
  • Ensure user access is available for the Central Business Configuration tenant. To access your SAP Central Business Configuration tenant.You will need to have your user account set up with the readFeatureActivationStatus & writeFeatureActivationStatus policies on your account with the connected SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant.
     
  •  On the dashboard, a list of features is provided and grouped by the product name. Select “SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI Capabilities”.
  • Click on Activate to proceed with the activation process. Once the activation is complete, ensure that the status is displayed as Active.
     
  • Assign named users through SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS) to enable access for authorized users. Navigate to SAP Cloud Identity Services.
    Under Users & Authorizations Select the user and assign the group “j4d_abap-enterprise-user”
  • In the tab Resources you will find a new tenant providing its Main URL (which will lead to the UI of the application).

Provide that URL to the developers so they can configure SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities for side-by-side use in ABAP Development tools for Eclipse or in ABAP Development tools for Visual Studio Code.

For Developers Using ABAP Development Tools (ADT) for Eclipse: Steps to configure SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI Capabilities.

Prerequisites

Your SAP administrator provides you with a URL that refers to the SAP BTP.

You have applied the relevant SAP notes below:

  • To enable ABAP development tools for Eclipse (ADT) for SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities refer to SAP Note 3752299 .
  • When working on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition also follow the instructions in the SAP Notes 3627794  and 3751297 .
  • To enable AI Capabilities for ATC Findings follow the SAP Note 3721223 - AI Capabilities for ATC findings - SAP for Me
  • If you are unfamiliar how to implement an SAP note with TCI instructions, please refer to https://me.sap.com/notes/3437407

Configure the SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities for Specific ABAP Projects

Note: ADT Client version 3.58.3 or higher is required for the new AI setup based on AI Units. Ensure that your Eclipse ADT plug-in is updated accordingly before proceeding with the configuration.

Home page for installation: https://tools.hana.ondemand.com/#abap
ADT client URL: https://tools.hana.ondemand.com/latest

To use the latest SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities in specific ABAP projects in your ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse.

  • In the Project Explorer, select the relevant ABAP project and Choose Properties from the context menu.
          
  • Select make the input available, select the Enable project-specific settings checkbox.
  • Beneath the Use Joule side-by-side radio button, enter the URL to the subscription of the central BTP service on the SAP BTP.
    Optional: Choose the Check Connection button to control if the connection works.
     
  • Confirm with Apply.

  • You can now use the SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities side-by-side in the selected ABAP project.
    Click here to explore what’s in it!

For Developers Using ABAP Development Tools (ADT) for Visual Studio Code: Steps to configure SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI Capabilities.

Prerequisites

Your SAP administrator provides you a URL that refers to the SAP BTP.

Configure the SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities for all ABAP Projects

As an ABAP developer, you want to use the latest SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities for all of your ABAP projects in your ADT client.

  1. To enable the SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities side-by-side, open the settings. To do this, use the File > Preferences >Settings menu.
  2. Navigate to the Extensions >ABAP Development settings group.
  3. Open the Adt>  Joule>  Url setting.
  4. Enter the URL for SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities to the subscription of the central BTP service on the SAP Business Technology Platform.

In ADT for Visual Studio Code, you can now use the SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities side-by-side in all of your supported ABAP projects.

Overview of SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities supported in SAP products including lower releases of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition

Currently using ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse, the following ABAP AI capabilities are supported in the different products and releases:

ABAP AI CAPABILITY

SAP BTP ABAP ENVIRONMENT

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025

 In addition, of course, all capabilities offered by your coding agent of choice are supported and driven by our ADT MCP Server.

With the introduction of the ADT MCP Server, developers can unlock a truly agentic ABAP development experience. The ADT MCP Server makes ABAP development tools easily accessible to AI systems, streamlining development tasks, simplifying workflows, and improving efficiency.

To learn more about the available MCP tools in ADT for eclipse, see:   Model Context Protocol Tools | SAP Help Portal

Furthermore, step by step, we will achieve a similar level of ABAP AI capabilities support in ADT for Visual Studio Code.

To learn more about the available MCP tools in ADT for Visual Studio Code, see: Model Context Protocol Tools | SAP Help Portal

 

With that, go and explore the re-invented ABAP AI offering!

 Coauthors,

 @karl_kessler @niloofar_naseri

entry.title: Building a Procurement Agent for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition with SAP Joule Studio – Part 3
entry.content:
TL;DR: This is the third and final post in a series about building a custom AI agent for Joule, specialized in creating purchase requisitions in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition through a single prompt. In this part, we walk through the steps to create Joule Agents in Joule Studio, using all the objects built in parts 1 and 2.

 

 

After creating a Joule Skill in part 2 of our series, we have reached the final step: creating the Joule Agent responsible for orchestrating the purchase requisition from a single prompt. We will explore how Joule Studio can help us create these agents and walk through some prompt engineering techniques to produce better results.

 

Understanding a Joule Agent

A Joule Agent is an AI agent that can be orchestrated, combining reasoning, planning, and automated execution to achieve business goals, using a collection of tools, which include Joule Skills. While a skill is better aligned with an atomic action (for example, calling an API to retrieve information about a business object), an agent is the “brain” that understands the intent, chooses which skills to use and in what order, and iterates until the task is complete.

 

Typical Structure of a Joule Agent

Here are the common building blocks of a Joule Agent created with Joule Studio:

  • Goals and persona: what the agent must achieve and with what behavior (e.g., purchasing assistant, cash flow analyst).
  • Available tools/skills: the set of Joule Skills and connectors the agent can invoke.
  • Planning and orchestration: logic to decide the sequence of steps, re-plan when needed, and handle exceptions.
  • Context and memory: ability to maintain state across steps and sessions, when allowed.
  • Input and output model: how the agent receives requests from the user or the system and standardizes the results.

 

When Should You Use a Joule Agent?

If you have been following our series up to this point, you may have wondered whether you should always wrap a Joule Skill inside a Joule Agent. The answer is: no. So, your next questions are probably: can a Joule Skill be consumed directly by the standard Joule Agent? And when should I create a custom one, and when should I not?

Joule Skills can indeed be consumed directly by the standard Joule agent.
